Military Apr 10, 2026

US Army Advances Bell & M1 In Bid To Outsource Future Helicopter Pilot Training

The final phase of Flight School Next is coming.

The Army's outsourcing of helicopter pilot training to commercial contractors could reshape military aviation workforce development and reduce government training costs while building private-sector aviation expertise.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

Here's What United Airlines' New Base Polaris Fare Actually Costs You In Lost Perks

What do the changes mean for the airline's business class passengers?

United's stripped-down Polaris base tier risks commoditizing premium cabin revenue by eroding the service differentiation that historically justified business-class pricing premiums.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 10, 2026

Earthset From the Lunar Far Side

The crew of NASA’s Artemis II mission captured extraordinary images of our home planet during their journey around the far side of the Moon.

Artemis II's lunar far-side imagery demonstrates NASA's advancing deep-space exploration capabilities essential for sustained human spaceflight beyond Earth orbit.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

Cluj-Napoca: the rapidly growing Romanian airport that is now eyeing US flights

If there is one airport that has benefited from the momentous change to travel habits in Eastern Europe…...

Cluj-Napoca's rapid growth reflects Eastern Europe's emerging role as a secondary hub, positioning Romania to capture transatlantic traffic diverted from congested Western European airports.

See AeroTime Hub →

Industry Apr 10, 2026

One global path to net zero aviation: Join us during Climate Week!

The aviation sector plays a vital role in connecting the world, supporting economic development, and enabling global mobility. At the same time, the international aviation community is working collectively to address its environmental impact and advance the transition toward a more sustainable...

The aviation industry is coordinating a unified global sustainability strategy rather than pursuing fragmented regional approaches to achieve net-zero emissions.

See ICAO Uniting Aviation →

PAI
Safety Apr 10, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation from investigating accidents after they occur to systematically preventing them through continuous risk identification and mitigation.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

At Maximum Range, This Is What Pushes Aircraft Like The Airbus A350 To Their Limits

Pushing the Airbus A350 to its maximum range is a risk, but for the furtherance of aviation history, it is likely one worth taking.

Operating at absolute maximum range severely degrades safety margins and fuel reserves, making such flights viable only for rare record attempts rather than routine operations.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 9, 2026

From 19 B-2 Spirits To 145 B-21 Raiders: How Much Bigger America's Stealth Bomber Fleet Is About To Get

A $4.5 billion production deal signal the Air Force's commitment to building its next-generation bomber...

The Air Force's shift from 19 B-2s to 145 B-21s represents a 660% fleet expansion, fundamentally reshaping long-range strike capability and defense industrial demand for decades.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Asiana Airlines to be Taken Over by Korean Air 2026

According to local news outlet Tomato News, Korean Air is planning to acquire the remaining 36.1% stake in Asiana Airlines. It owns 63.9% currently and much control in the management of it. But it wants to form a single airline structure, gradually removing the airline as a brand, starting in...

Korean Air's acquisition of Asiana represents a major consolidation in Northeast Asian aviation, likely reshaping regional competition and reducing fleet redundancy across the carrier's network.

See Travel Radar →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

Federal Aviation Administration's Crippling Shortage Risks Mass Flight Delays & Safety

More than 5,100 accidents had a narrative that air traffic control was a factor or probable cause.

The FAA's staffing crisis has already contributed to over 5,100 documented accidents, representing a direct safety hazard that extends far beyond scheduling inconveniences.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Regulatory Apr 10, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

The SMS Declaration of Compliance certifies an operator's safety management system meets FAA standards, serving as verification that risk mitigation processes are integrated throughout flight operations.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Airbus Delivered 60 New Planes In March, But Overall Q1 Output Lags 16% From Last Year

Pratt & Whitney engine delays are now the clearest drag on Airbus’ 2026 delivery performance.

Pratt & Whitney's engine production bottlenecks are becoming the primary constraint on Airbus' ability to meet 2026 delivery targets, not airframer capacity.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Ouch: American Airlines Joins 4 Other US Carriers In Raising Checked Bag Fees

American Airlines becomes the latest US carrier to increase checked bag fees for basic economy tickets.

Coordinated fee increases across competing carriers reduce consumer choice and collectively shift ancillary revenue expectations upward industry-wide.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

Beech Bash to feature aerobatic Bonanza reunion

Organizers expect up to a third of the roughly 30 aerobatic Beechcraft Bonanzas still in service will gather in Madisonville, Kentucky, April 23 to 26 for the fifth annual Beech Bash in the Bluegrass. A long weekend of camaraderie, food, concerts, airshows, and presentations by well-known aviators...

Aerobatic Bonanza operators are consolidating around specialized community events, signaling niche but persistent demand for high-performance piston aircraft modifications.

See AOPA News →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

The Growth of Regional Airlines this 2026

Regional carriers are gaining traction as major airlines cut capacity or reshuffle their networks due to U.S.–Iran conflict and the surge in fuel prices. This is creating opportunities for smaller operators to serve underserved markets with short‑haul flights. New Routes and Network Expansion in...

Geopolitical disruption and fuel volatility are fragmenting airline networks, enabling regional carriers to capture capacity gaps in point-to-point markets that major carriers are abandoning.

See Travel Radar →

▶15:03
Safety Apr 10, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Assurance

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety assurance, which evaluates the continued effectiveness of implemented risk control strategies and supports the identification of new hazards. Whether you're an owner, operator or part of a small aviation team, this guide provides valuable insights and tools to enhance your safety management practices and ensure a safer operational environment.

Safety assurance in SMS enables small operators to systematically verify risk controls work as designed while staying alert to emerging hazards.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Why Airlines Prefer Window Shades To Be Open During Taxi, Takeoff, & Landing

This is a simple safety concept, but not all airlines agree.

Open window shades during critical phases enable crew and passengers to detect external hazards and emergencies faster, reducing response time when seconds matter most.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Indoor Testing Facilities available at the NASA Unmanned Autonomy Research Complex (NUARC)

Windshaper A large WindShaper fan array is available for dynamic low-speed and hovering flight research. The WindShaper is ideal for generating arbitrary wind gradients and wind gusts via a simple Python API. A companion WindProbe is also available for quick surveys of flows. The WindProbe utilizes...

NASA's NUARC facility enables controlled atmospheric testing of autonomous aircraft through programmable wind simulation, reducing development costs and risks before real-world validation.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

I Am Artemis: Dan Florez

Listen to this audio excerpt from Dan Florez, test director for NASA’s Exploration Ground Systems Program: At 1:47 a.m. EST November 16, 2022, as the Artemis I engines ignited, NASA astronaut Reid Wiseman and Dan Florez, NASA test director for the agency’s Exploration Ground Systems, watched from...

Ground systems directors like Florez are critical to launch success, managing complex infrastructure that enables crewed missions beyond Earth orbit.

See NASA →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Frontier Airlines Pilots Slam Brakes On Busy LAX Taxiway: “2 Trucks Just Cut Us Off”

The near-miss has sparked concerns about vehicle transponders, following the deadly Air Canada crash last month.

Recurring ground vehicle incursions at major hubs suggest transponder mandates and upgraded detection systems may become regulatory imperatives following safety incidents.

See Simple Flying →

▶16:49
General Aviation Apr 10, 2026

FAA Aviation Safety at Oshkosh

Go behind the scenes at EAA AirVenture with the FAA's Aviation Safety organization. Meet the people who help ensure safety at this event and provide essential information resources to the general aviation community.

The FAA's on-site safety presence at AirVenture demonstrates how regulatory agencies actively engage with grassroots aviators to disseminate safety guidance beyond traditional enforcement channels.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

The Airbus A350 Is Known As The World's Extra Widebody, But Soon It Won’t Be

The Boeing 777X is set to be the widest passenger jet in production, although the A350-1000 appears set to retain the record for maximum exit limit.

Boeing's 777X will overtake Airbus's A350 as the widest production aircraft, reshaping competitive dynamics in the ultra-large widebody market.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Indoor Testing Facilities available at the NASA Unmanned Autonomy Research Complex (NUARC)

WindShaper A large WindShaper fan array is available for dynamic low-speed and hovering flight research. The WindShaper is ideal for generating arbitrary wind gradients and wind gusts via a simple Python API. A companion WindProbe is also available for quick surveys of flows. The WindProbe utilizes...

NASA's NUARC WindShaper facility enables researchers to simulate complex real-world wind conditions indoors, accelerating development cycles for autonomous aircraft and eVTOL platforms without outdoor weather dependencies.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

United Airlines increases checked bag fees

United Airlines has become the latest airline to increase the checked baggage fee, effective immediately, in response to rising fuel costs. Other airlines, including Delta Air Lines, Qantas, and Air Canada, have also increased ticket prices to manage fuel shortages. Check-in luggage cost in United...

Rising fuel costs are triggering industry-wide fee increases across major carriers, signaling broader cost pressures that may extend beyond baggage fees to other ancillary charges.

See Travel Radar →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

EAA Aviation Foundation Awards 127 Scholarships for 2025-26

Flight training and post-secondary awards total more than $800,000.

EAA's scholarship expansion demonstrates the industry's commitment to pipeline development as pilot shortages persist across commercial and general aviation sectors.

See AVweb →

Industry Apr 10, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-flight systems offers near-term capacity gains without infrastructure expansion, addressing the critical bottleneck between surging demand and environmental constraints.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Starstruck

The Artemis II crew captured this photo of our galaxy, the Milky Way, on April 7, 2026. The Milky Way’s elegant spiral structure is dominated by just two arms wrapping off the ends of a central bar of stars. Spanning more than 100,000 light-years, Earth is located along one of the galaxy’s spiral...

Artemis II's orbital perspective demonstrates how space exploration missions provide unprecedented astronomical observation capabilities that advance our understanding of galactic structure.

See NASA →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 9, 2026

2015 Lockwood AirCam over the Palisades Reservoir.

The Lockwood AirCam's continued presence in recreational aviation demonstrates the enduring market viability of innovative light-sport aircraft designs for backcountry and scenic operations.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

House Set To Vote on ALERT Act Next Week

Measure would direct FAA rulemaking on collision-avoidance systems, airspace procedures and military operations.

Congressional mandate could accelerate FAA regulatory timelines for collision-avoidance mandates and military-civilian airspace integration, reshaping equipment retrofit requirements across the fleet.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

Justice wants $110.3M to boost zero-trust cybersecurity in fiscal 2027

After falling victim to the SolarWinds breach in 2020, Justice has worked to implement a zero-trust cybersecurity architecture, but saw its funding for cyber cut by $108 million in fiscal 2024.

Aviation operators dependent on DOJ systems for compliance and security oversight face increased vulnerability as federal cybersecurity investments face budget constraints despite demonstrated breach history.

See FedScoop →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Watching the Artemis II Mission Unfold at JPL’s Space Flight Operations Facility

Description Staff at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California watch the agency’s Artemis II mission unfold soon after launch on April 1, 2026, at the Space Flight Operations Facility, which operates the Deep Space Network (DSN). The DSN comprises of three complexes in Goldstone,...

NASA's Deep Space Network infrastructure, managed by JPL's Space Flight Operations Facility, remains critical for maintaining real-time communication with lunar missions beyond Earth orbit.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

The Deep Space Network Acquires Artemis II Signal

Description A graphical representation of the Deep Space Network’s radio frequency antennas indicate signal acquisition from NASA’s Artemis II mission to the Moon on April 1, 2026, inside the Space Flight Operations Facility at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California. Two antennas...

NASA's Deep Space Network successfully tracked Artemis II's lunar trajectory, demonstrating critical ground infrastructure capability for sustained human spaceflight beyond Earth orbit.

See NASA →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

FAA Pilot Population Climbs Again In 2025

Latest Civil Airmen Statistics show the strongest gains from student pilots.

Student pilot enrollment growth signals potential relief for the industry's chronic pilot shortage, though sustainability depends on completion rates and airline hiring demand.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

The Deep Space Network Acquires Artemis II Signal

Description The acquisition of the radio frequency signal from the Artemis II crewed mission to the Moon by NASA’s Deep Space Network (DSN) is indicated by the peak in the data signal shown on the top computer screen. Soon after the mission’s launch on April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT, NASA’s Near...

NASA's Deep Space Network successfully locked onto Artemis II's signal shortly after launch, confirming real-time communication capability for lunar missions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Watching Over the Deep Space Network Before Artemis II Signal Acquisition

Description Blanca Renteria, Artemis Deep Space Network (DSN) operations chief, monitors data at the Space Flight Operations Facility at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California shortly after Artemis II launched from the agency’s Kennedy Space Flight Center in Florida on April 1,...

NASA's Deep Space Network operations teams must maintain real-time tracking and data acquisition capabilities for lunar missions, establishing critical ground infrastructure dependencies that aerospace operators should understand for future deep space communications.

See NASA →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

AI risk management deadline hits federal agencies. Not all were ready.

DHS and DOJ are among the agencies lagging behind those that have publicly posted updated AI inventories, per OMB requirements, and outlined changes to FedScoop.

Federal aviation agencies managing critical air traffic control and safety systems may face compliance gaps in AI governance, potentially affecting certification and operational oversight standards.

See FedScoop →
